findings.finding_title: S3 Buckets Missing Block Public Access Across Multiple Accounts
findings.root_cause_narrative: S3 Block Public Access was not enabled at the bucket level, likely because buckets were provisioned without enforcing account-wide public access controls or organizational guardrails. The absence of a default-deny baseline allows individual buckets to remain exposed.
findings.situation_narrative: Four S3 buckets across three accounts (acme-prod, acme-dev, acme-legacy) have Block Public Access disabled, leaving them open to public access via ACLs or bucket policies. The flagged bucket acme-prod-logs holds logging data and lacks all four Block Public Access settings. Because production, development, and legacy environments are affected, the exposure spans varied data sensitivity levels.
findings.consequence_narrative: An attacker or unauthorized party could read or exfiltrate stored data, including log data that may contain sensitive operational details. Public write access, if also permitted, could allow tampering or data injection.
findings.access_required: No authenticated access is required if a public ACL or bucket policy grants access; an unauthenticated internet user could reach the bucket directly.

raw.check_title: Ensure S3 bucket does not allow public access
raw.description: This control checks whether Amazon S3 buckets have Block Public Access enabled.
raw.risk: Public S3 buckets expose data to the internet. Attackers can exfiltrate sensitive data.
raw.remediation_text: Enable S3 Block Public Access settings at the bucket level for all four settings.

findings.finding_title: S3 Buckets Missing Default Encryption Across acme-prod and acme-legacy
findings.root_cause_narrative: These buckets were created without an explicit default encryption configuration, likely predating organisational encryption standards or being provisioned manually without enforcing a baseline policy.
findings.situation_narrative: Two S3 buckets across the acme-prod and acme-legacy accounts have no default encryption configured, including acme-prod-archive. Objects uploaded to these buckets are stored without server-side encryption unless the client specifies it per request. This deviates from CIS-2.0 control 2.1.1, which requires default encryption on all S3 buckets.
findings.consequence_narrative: If an attacker gains read access to the underlying storage or a backup snapshot, object data would be exposed in plaintext, and the lack of enforced encryption increases the risk of non-compliant data handling for archived records.
findings.access_required: An attacker would need read access to the bucket contents or to the underlying storage layer, typically through compromised credentials or an overly permissive bucket policy.

raw.check_title: Ensure S3 bucket has default encryption enabled
raw.description: This control checks whether default encryption is enabled on Amazon S3 buckets.
raw.risk: Without default encryption, objects are stored in plaintext.
raw.remediation_text: Enable default encryption on the S3 bucket using SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS.

LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

findings.finding_title: IAM Users with Console Access Lack MFA Protection
findings.root_cause_narrative: IAM users were provisioned with console passwords but MFA enrollment was never enforced through policy or onboarding controls. Without a service control policy or IAM condition requiring MFA, console access defaults to single-factor authentication.
findings.situation_narrative: The IAM user john.smith has console sign-in enabled but no MFA device registered. This condition affects 4 IAM users across 2 accounts (acme-prod, acme-legacy), each able to authenticate to the AWS Management Console with only a password.
findings.consequence_narrative: A compromised or guessed password grants full console access tied to the user's permissions, enabling resource manipulation, data exfiltration, or privilege escalation. The presence of affected users in a production account increases the potential blast radius.
findings.access_required: An attacker needs only valid console credentials (password), obtainable via phishing, credential stuffing, or reuse, with no second factor to defeat.

raw.check_title: Ensure MFA is enabled for all IAM users with console access
raw.remediation_text: Enable MFA for the IAM user via the AWS Console or CLI.

LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

raw.remediation_text: Enable MFA for the IAM user via the AWS Console or CLI.

findings.finding_title: Root Account Missing MFA in acme-prod
findings.root_cause_narrative: The AWS account root user was created without a hardware or virtual MFA device being subsequently enrolled. This commonly results from initial account setup steps skipping MFA configuration and a lack of enforced governance over the root identity.
findings.situation_narrative: The root user for account 'acme-prod' has no MFA device configured, meaning authentication relies solely on the root password. This affects a single resource (the account summary), but the root identity holds unrestricted privileges across all services in the account.
findings.consequence_narrative: If the root credentials are phished, leaked, or brute-forced, an attacker gains complete control of the account with no second authentication factor to stop them. This could enable data exfiltration, resource destruction, billing abuse, and lockout of legitimate administrators.
findings.access_required: An attacker needs only the root account password, obtainable via phishing, credential reuse, or a leak, with no second factor required.

raw.check_title: Ensure MFA is enabled for the root account
raw.description: Checks whether MFA is enabled for the AWS account root user.
raw.risk: The root account has unrestricted access to all AWS services. Without MFA it is vulnerable to credential theft.
raw.remediation_text: Enable a hardware or virtual MFA device for the root account.

findings.finding_title: IAM Access Key for svc-deploy Exceeds 90-Day Rotation Threshold (134 Days Old)
findings.root_cause_narrative: The access key for the svc-deploy IAM user was created and never rotated, likely because no automated rotation process or lifecycle policy is enforced for service account credentials. Long-lived keys often persist when they are embedded in deployment pipelines or scripts that make rotation operationally inconvenient.
findings.situation_narrative: The active access key (AKIAI...) belonging to IAM user svc-deploy in account 'acme-prod' is 134 days old, exceeding the 90-day rotation policy required by CIS 2.0 control 1.14. This finding affects a single resource. As a service account used for deployments, this key may be distributed across CI/CD systems or configuration files, increasing its exposure surface.
findings.consequence_narrative: If the key is leaked through logs, source control, or a compromised pipeline host, an attacker could use it to authenticate as svc-deploy and perform actions within the scope of its permissions. The extended key age increases the window during which a compromised credential remains valid and undetected.
findings.access_required: An attacker would need to obtain the plaintext access key and secret, typically by accessing a CI/CD system, source repository, log file, or host where the credential is stored.

raw.check_title: Ensure IAM user access keys are rotated every 90 days or less
raw.description: This control checks whether IAM user access keys are rotated within 90 days.
raw.risk: Long-lived access keys increase the risk of credential compromise.
raw.remediation_text: Rotate IAM user access keys and delete old keys.

audit_trail.reason: Grouped 1 instance(s) of check_id='iam_user_access_key_age_90_days' into one output row
LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

findings.finding_title: IAM Password Policy Does Not Require Lowercase Letters in Account acme-prod
findings.root_cause_narrative: The IAM account password policy was either left at its default configuration or defined without enabling the lowercase character requirement. This typically occurs when password complexity rules are set manually without full coverage of all character-class options.
findings.situation_narrative: The IAM password policy for account 'acme-prod' does not enforce the inclusion of at least one lowercase letter in user passwords. This affects the single account-wide password policy resource and reduces overall password complexity for all IAM users authenticating with passwords. The configuration deviates from CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark 2.0 control 1.8.
findings.consequence_narrative: Weaker password complexity increases the feasibility of brute-force and dictionary attacks against IAM user credentials, potentially enabling unauthorized console access. The actual exposure depends on the strength of other policy controls such as minimum length and MFA enforcement.
findings.access_required: An attacker would need network access to the AWS console login and an opportunity to guess or brute-force a targeted IAM user's password.

raw.check_title: Ensure IAM password policy requires at least one lowercase letter
raw.description: Checks whether the IAM password policy requires lowercase letters.

audit_trail.reason: Grouped 1 instance(s) of check_id='iam_password_policy_lowercase' into one output row
LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

findings.finding_title: Unrestricted SSH (port 22) Ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 on EC2 Security Groups
findings.root_cause_narrative: Security group rules were configured with an overly permissive source range of 0.0.0.0/0 on TCP port 22, typically to simplify administrative access during setup or troubleshooting. Such temporary allowances often persist into production because they are not revisited or governed by least-privilege standards.
findings.situation_narrative: Two security groups across the acme-prod and acme-legacy accounts permit unrestricted inbound SSH access from any IP address on the internet. The flagged resource sg-0prod1234 (web-servers, Environment=prod) in ap-southeast-2 exposes port 22 to 0.0.0.0/0, directly attaching internet-reachable SSH to production workloads.
findings.consequence_narrative: Internet-wide SSH exposure invites continuous brute-force and credential-stuffing attempts, and a successful login grants shell access to the underlying EC2 instances. From there an attacker could move laterally, exfiltrate data, or use the host as a foothold into the production environment.
findings.access_required: No prior access is needed beyond internet connectivity and valid or guessed SSH credentials, as the port is reachable by any host on the internet.

raw.check_title: Ensure no security groups allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to port 22
raw.description: Checks whether security groups allow unrestricted inbound access on TCP port 22 (SSH).
raw.risk: Unrestricted SSH access allows anyone to attempt brute-force login to EC2 instances.
raw.remediation_text: Restrict SSH ingress to specific trusted IP ranges. Remove the 0.0.0.0/0 rule.

LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

findings.finding_title: EC2 Instance i-0aabbccdd Does Not Enforce IMDSv2
findings.root_cause_narrative: The instance was launched with metadata options defaulting to optional token usage (HttpTokens set to optional), allowing both IMDSv1 and IMDSv2 requests. This commonly occurs when instances are provisioned from older launch templates or AMIs that predate IMDSv2 enforcement defaults.
findings.situation_narrative: EC2 instance i-0aabbccdd in account 'acme-prod' (region ap-southeast-2) permits unauthenticated IMDSv1 access to the instance metadata service. The instance does not require session tokens (HttpTokens is not set to required), leaving the legacy metadata endpoint reachable. This affects a single resource in a production account.
findings.consequence_narrative: An attacker exploiting a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in an application on the instance could retrieve the attached IAM role credentials via IMDSv1 with no token. Those credentials could then be used to access AWS resources within the scope of the instance's role, potentially enabling data exposure or lateral movement.
findings.access_required: An attacker needs the ability to trigger outbound requests from the instance, typically via an SSRF flaw in a hosted application, rather than direct host access.

raw.check_title: Ensure EC2 instances use IMDSv2
raw.description: Checks whether EC2 instances are configured to use Instance Metadata Service Version 2.
raw.risk: IMDSv1 is susceptible to SSRF attacks that can expose IAM credentials.
raw.remediation_text: Enforce IMDSv2 on all EC2 instances by setting HttpTokens to required.

audit_trail.reason: Grouped 1 instance(s) of check_id='ec2_instance_imdsv2_enabled' into one output row
LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

findings.root_cause_narrative: EBS encryption was not enabled at volume creation, and the account lacks a default EBS encryption policy to enforce it automatically. As a result, volumes were provisioned without server-side encryption.
findings.situation_narrative: Three EBS volumes in the acme-prod account (region ap-southeast-2) were found without encryption at rest, including vol-0aaa1111. Data stored on these volumes, along with any snapshots derived from them, is written in plaintext. This conflicts with CIS-2.0 control 2.2.1 and NIST 800-53 SC-28.
findings.consequence_narrative: If the underlying storage hardware, a snapshot, or a volume copy is accessed by an unauthorised party, sensitive data could be read directly without needing application-level access. This may result in data exposure and compliance violations.
findings.access_required: An attacker would need access to the underlying storage media, an exported volume, or a snapshot copy, typically via compromised AWS credentials or insider access rather than network-only access.

findings.finding_title: RDS Instance 'prod-postgres' Lacks Encryption at Rest
findings.root_cause_narrative: The RDS instance was provisioned without enabling storage encryption, which must be selected at creation time and cannot be toggled on an existing unencrypted instance. This typically results from default settings or omitting the encryption option during deployment.
findings.situation_narrative: The production PostgreSQL instance 'prod-postgres' in account 'acme-prod' (ap-southeast-2) stores its data, automated backups, snapshots, and read replicas without encryption at rest. This affects a single resource and falls short of CIS-2.0 (2.3.1) and NIST-800-53 (SC-28) requirements for data-at-rest protection.
findings.consequence_narrative: If the underlying storage, snapshots, or backups are accessed by an unauthorised party, production database contents would be readable in plaintext, risking exposure of sensitive customer or business data. The lack of encryption also undermines compliance attestations tied to CIS and NIST controls.
findings.access_required: An attacker would need access to the underlying storage media, exported snapshots, or AWS account permissions allowing snapshot copy or restore operations.

raw.check_title: Ensure RDS instances are encrypted at rest
raw.remediation_text: Enable encryption at rest for the RDS instance by creating an encrypted read replica and promoting it.

audit_trail.reason: Grouped 1 instance(s) of check_id='rds_instance_storage_encrypted' into one output row
LLM enrichment completed (attempt 1). Provider: bedrock_mantle. Model: anthropic.claude-opus-4-8.

findings.finding_title: Production PostgreSQL RDS Instance Publicly Accessible from the Internet
findings.root_cause_narrative: The RDS instance prod-postgres was provisioned or modified with the 'publicly accessible' flag enabled, assigning it a public endpoint. This typically results from default deployment settings or a temporary access need that was never reverted.
findings.situation_narrative: The RDS DB instance prod-postgres in account 'acme-prod' (region ap-southeast-2) is configured as publicly accessible, exposing its endpoint to the internet. This affects a single production database resource. Public reachability widens the attack surface beyond the intended VPC boundary, leaving exposure dependent on security group and network ACL controls.
findings.consequence_narrative: An internet-facing production database invites brute-force authentication attempts, exploitation of database vulnerabilities, and potential data exfiltration. Compromise of a production PostgreSQL instance could expose sensitive customer or business data and disrupt dependent services.
findings.access_required: A network-level attacker with no prior access could reach the database endpoint over the internet; exploitation would then require valid or guessed credentials or an unpatched database vulnerability.

findings.finding_title: VPC Flow Logging Disabled on Production VPC vpc-0prod5678
findings.root_cause_narrative: VPC flow logging is not enabled by default on AWS VPCs and must be explicitly configured. This VPC was provisioned without a flow log configuration directing traffic metadata to CloudWatch Logs or S3.
findings.situation_narrative: The production VPC vpc-0prod5678 in account 'acme-prod' (region ap-southeast-2) has no flow logging enabled. Network traffic metadata for this VPC is therefore not being captured. This finding affects a single resource.
findings.consequence_narrative: Without flow logs, security teams cannot reconstruct network activity during incident investigations, hampering detection of lateral movement, data exfiltration, or unauthorised connections. The absence of this telemetry also weakens compliance posture against CIS and NIST logging controls.
findings.access_required: No direct attacker access is required; this is a visibility gap that degrades the ability to investigate any compromise of resources within the VPC.

raw.check_title: Ensure VPC flow logging is enabled in all VPCs
raw.description: Checks whether VPC flow logs are enabled for each VPC.
raw.risk: Without flow logs, network traffic is not captured and security incidents cannot be investigated.
raw.remediation_text: Enable VPC flow logs for all VPCs and send logs to CloudWatch Logs or S3.

findings.finding_title: CloudTrail Trail 'management-trail' Not Configured for Multi-Region Logging
findings.root_cause_narrative: The trail was provisioned as a single-region trail, capturing API activity only in ap-southeast-2. This commonly occurs when a trail is created through region-scoped tooling or default console settings without enabling the multi-region option.
findings.situation_narrative: The CloudTrail trail 'management-trail' in account 'acme-prod' is not enabled across all AWS regions. As a result, API calls made in regions other than ap-southeast-2 are not recorded by this trail, leaving gaps in audit coverage.
findings.consequence_narrative: Activity in unlogged regions, including resource creation, privilege changes, or data access by an attacker, would go unrecorded and undetected, undermining incident response and forensic investigations. Attackers frequently operate in unused regions specifically to evade region-scoped logging.
findings.access_required: An attacker would need valid AWS credentials or a foothold in the account to perform API actions in non-logged regions while evading detection.

raw.check_title: Ensure CloudTrail is enabled in all regions
raw.description: Checks whether CloudTrail is enabled in all AWS regions.
raw.risk: Without multi-region CloudTrail, API calls in non-logged regions go undetected.
raw.remediation_text: Update the CloudTrail trail to be multi-region.

findings.finding_title: CloudWatch Log Group /aws/lambda/prod-api Missing Retention Policy
findings.root_cause_narrative: CloudWatch log groups default to indefinite retention unless a retention period is explicitly configured. This log group was created without a retention policy being set, most likely because the Lambda function's log group was auto-created without accompanying infrastructure-as-code controls.
findings.situation_narrative: The CloudWatch log group /aws/lambda/prod-api in account 'acme-prod' (ap-southeast-2) has no retention policy applied. This affects a single resource and means log data accumulates indefinitely with no automated expiry. The finding is informational and primarily relates to logging hygiene and cost optimisation.
findings.consequence_narrative: Indefinite log retention drives steadily increasing storage costs over time and may extend the window in which sensitive data captured in logs is retained beyond compliance or data-minimisation requirements. There is no direct security exposure from this configuration alone.
findings.access_required: No attacker access is required; this is a cost and data-lifecycle hygiene issue rather than an exploitable vulnerability.

raw.check_title: Ensure CloudWatch log groups have retention policies set
raw.description: Checks whether CloudWatch log groups have a retention period set.
raw.risk: Without retention policies, log groups accumulate data indefinitely increasing storage costs.
raw.remediation_text: Set a retention policy on the CloudWatch log group.
